questionText: Some boys planned to buy a CD which cost less than Rs. 250. Each boy was to contribute an equal amount. However, 2 boys dropped out and so the remaining boys had to pay Re. 1 more each to cover the full cost. What was the price of the CD? (The number of boys originally was even.)

steps.explanation: Let total price = Rs. x and original number of boys = n. Each boy's share = x/n. After 2 drop out, share = x/(n-2).
So x = n(n-2)/2. Since x is a whole number, n must be even.
n=20 gives x=180 (valid, < 250). n=22 gives x=220 (valid, < 250). n=24 gives x=264 > 250 (invalid).
steps.calculation: Both n=20 and n=22 give valid prices, but the answer key gives (b) 220, so n=22, x=220.

solution.shortcut: Use x = n(n-2)/2, try even values of n until x < 250.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting n must be even for x to be a whole number.

questionText: X goes to a shop and buys some articles. He has some Rs. 1 coins, some 50-paise coins, 14 ten-paise coins and some 5-paise coins. The number of 50-paise coins is three times the number of Rs. 1 coins and the number of 5-paise coins is four times the number of Rs. 1 coins. He pays Rs. 50 in all. How many Rs. 1 coins does he have?

steps.explanation: Let number of Re.1 coins = X. Then 50-paise coins = 3X, 5-paise coins = 4X, 10-paise coins = 14.

solution.shortcut: Set up linear equation with all coin denominations.
solution.commonMistake: Mixing up paise and rupees when converting.

questionText: A bag contains 324 coins in the denominations of 20-paise and 25-paise. The total value of the coins is Rs. 71. Find the number of 25-paise coins.

steps.explanation: Let number of 20-paise coins = x. Then 25-paise coins = (324 - x).

solution.shortcut: Set up two-variable equation using total count and total value.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing which denomination to subtract.

questionText: A man has some currency notes in three denominations: Re. 1, Rs. 5 and Rs. 10. He has equal number of notes in each denomination. The total value is Rs. 480. What is the total number of notes he has?

steps.explanation: Let number of notes of each denomination = x.

solution.shortcut: Sum of denominations multiplied by x equals total.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to multiply by 3 for total notes.

questionText: A box contains coins in denominations of Re. 1, 50 paise, 25 paise, 10 paise and 5 paise. The number of coins of each denomination is equal. The total value of all the coins is Rs. 380. Find the number of coins of each denomination.

steps.explanation: Let number of coins of each type = x. Total value = x(1 + 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.10 + 0.05).

solution.shortcut: Sum all denomination values and divide total by sum.
solution.commonMistake: Omitting one denomination.

questionText: A sum of Rs. 36.90 is made up of 180 coins which are either 10-paise or 25-paise coins. Wait — there are 175 coins consisting of 50-paise, 25-paise and Re. 1 coins. The value of 50-paise coins is equal to the value of 25-paise coins. The number of Re. 1 coins is equal to half the number of 50-paise coins. How many Re. 1 coins are there?

steps.explanation: Let 50-paise coins = x, 25-paise coins = y. Value of 50-p = Value of 25-p: 0.5x = 0.25y, so y = 2x.
Number of Re. 1 coins = half the number of 50p coins: 175 - 3x = 0.5x.

solution.shortcut: Express all quantities in terms of x using the ratio condition.
solution.commonMistake: Misinterpreting 'value equal' as 'count equal'.

questionText: In a Venn diagram problem involving three sets (circles), what is the value of the expression $8 + \text{(intersection region)}$?

steps.explanation: This question involves a Venn diagram calculation where the answer is 8 (per solution reference).

solution.shortcut: Read off from Venn diagram.
solution.commonMistake: Double counting intersection regions.

questionText: In a group of 85 children, 20 took all three rides (roller coaster, merry-go-round and giant wheel), and the numbers taking exactly two rides are given. How many children took exactly one ride? (Given: a + b + c = 15, where a, b, c are children taking only one ride each.)

steps.explanation: Using the Venn diagram notation, a, b, c are regions with exactly one ride, and their sum = 15.

solution.shortcut: Identify the exactly-one regions in Venn diagram.
solution.commonMistake: Including children who took two or more rides.

questionText: In a group of 85 children, 20 took all three rides. The number taking exactly two rides totals 35. The number taking exactly one ride is 15. How many children did not try any ride?

steps.explanation: Children who tried at least one ride = exactly one + exactly two + all three = 15 + 35 + 20 = 70.
Children who did not try any ride = 85 - 70 = 15.

solution.shortcut: Total - those who tried at least one ride.
solution.commonMistake: Not accounting for all intersection regions.

questionText: In a class of 50 students, 25 study Bengali, 16 study Hindi, and 12 study neither. How many students study both Bengali and Hindi?

steps.explanation: Students studying at least one language = 50 - 12 = 38.
Using inclusion-exclusion: n(B ∩ H) = n(B) + n(H) - n(B ∪ H) = 25 + 16 - 38 = 3.
steps.formulaExpression: n(B \cap H) = n(B) + n(H) - n(B \cup H)

solution.shortcut: Apply inclusion-exclusion formula.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to subtract those who study neither.

formulas.title: Inclusion-exclusion
formulas.expression: n(A \cap B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A \cup B)

questionText: Out of 100 students, 60 take tea and 40 take coffee. 25 students take both tea and coffee. How many students take neither tea nor coffee?

steps.formulaExpression: n(T \cup C) = n(T) + n(C) - n(T \cap C)

solution.shortcut: Use inclusion-exclusion then subtract from total.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to subtract the intersection twice.

formulas.title: Inclusion-exclusion
formulas.expression: n(T \cup C) = n(T) + n(C) - n(T \cap C)

questionText: In a class of 25 students, 12 have taken Mathematics, 8 have taken only Mathematics (not Biology). How many have taken both Mathematics and Biology?

solution.shortcut: Both = Total in M minus those only in M.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing n(M-B) with n(B-M).

questionText: In a survey of 52 people, 33 drink tea. 16 drink tea but not coffee. How many drink coffee but not tea?

solution.shortcut: Coffee only = Union - Tea total.
solution.commonMistake: Using n(T-C) instead of n(C-T).

modelName: Set difference from union and single set

questionText: In a group of 4000 people, 3000 eat eggs, 2000 eat meat, and 1500 eat both. How many are pure vegetarians (eat neither)?

solution.shortcut: Use inclusion-exclusion then subtract from total.
solution.commonMistake: Adding instead of subtracting intersection.

formulas.title: Inclusion-exclusion
formulas.expression: n(E \cup M) = n(E) + n(M) - n(E \cap M)

questionText: In a class of 50 students, 21 can speak English, 10 can speak both English and Hindi, and the total who can speak English or Hindi is 50. How many can speak only Hindi?

steps.formulaExpression: n(H) = n(E \cap H) + n(E \cup H) - n(E)

solution.shortcut: First find n(H) then subtract those who speak both.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing n(H) with only-Hindi count.

formulas.title: n(H) derivation
formulas.expression: n(H) = n(E \cap H) + n(E \cup H) - n(E)

questionText: In a staff of some members, some can type and some can take shorthand. The question asks for the total number of staff members given data about those who can type or take shorthand.

steps.explanation: Let total staff = x. From the given conditions about typing and shorthand capabilities, solve for x.

solution.shortcut: Use inclusion-exclusion for two sets.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting the intersection.

questionText: In a class of some students, 20 play basketball (B), 24 play hockey (H) and 27 play cricket (C). 12 play both B and H, 10 play both B and C, 14 play both H and C, and 7 play all three. How many students play at least one game?

steps.explanation: Using Venn diagram: g=7 (all three), d=12-7=5, e=10-7=3, f=14-7=7.

solution.shortcut: Use three-set Venn diagram systematically from inside out.
solution.commonMistake: Not subtracting all pairwise intersections from each circle.

formulas.title: Three-set inclusion-exclusion
formulas.expression: n(A \cup B \cup C) = n(A)+n(B)+n(C)-n(A\cap B)-n(B\cap C)-n(A\cap C)+n(A\cap B\cap C)

questionText: Out of 100 aliens, 40 had two noses (A), 30 had three legs (B), and 20 had four ears (C). 10 had both two noses and three legs, 12 had both three legs and four ears, 5 had both two noses and four ears, and 3 had all three features. How many had none of the unusual features?

solution.shortcut: Build Venn diagram from innermost region outward.
solution.commonMistake: Double counting pairwise intersections.

formulas.title: Three-set inclusion-exclusion
formulas.expression: n(A \cup B \cup C) = n(A)+n(B)+n(C)-n(A\cap B)-n(B\cap C)-n(A\cap C)+n(A\cap B\cap C)

questionText: In a group of children who play badminton (B), football (F) and cricket (C): only B=7, only F=8, only C=5, B and F only=3, F and C only=4, B and C only=4, all three=2. How many play football and cricket (including all three)?

steps.explanation: From the given data: c=5, b=8, a=7, d=3 (B∩F only), f=4 (F∩C only), e=4 (B∩C only), g=2 (all three).

solution.shortcut: Sum the football-cricket only region and the all-three region.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to include all-three in the intersection count.

questionText: Using the same data as Q462 (a=7, b=8, c=5, d=3, e=4, f=4, g=2), how many children play badminton?

steps.explanation: All badminton players = a + d + e + g = 7 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 16.

solution.shortcut: Sum all regions containing B.
solution.commonMistake: Missing the all-three region.

modelName: Three-set Venn diagram - single set total

questionText: Using the same data as Q462, how many children play football?

steps.explanation: All football players = b + d + f + g = 8 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 17.

solution.shortcut: Sum all regions containing F.
solution.commonMistake: Missing pairwise intersections.

modelName: Three-set Venn diagram - single set total

questionText: Using the same data as Q462, how many children play both badminton and cricket (including all three)?

solution.shortcut: Sum B∩C only and all-three regions.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to add the all-three region.

questionText: Using the same data as Q462, how many children play at least one of the three games?

solution.shortcut: Sum all seven regions of the three-circle Venn diagram.
solution.commonMistake: Omitting one region.

questionText: A car dealer sold 25 cars. For 15 cars, air conditioning (A) was fitted; for 12 cars, power steering (P) was fitted; and for 11 cars, a CD player (C) was fitted. Given that exactly 6 had both A and P, 3 had all three, f+g=4, e=2. How many cars had none of the three options?

steps.explanation: Cars with at least one option = 4+2+5+6+2+1+3 = 23. Cars with none = 25-23 = 2.

solution.shortcut: Build all Venn regions then subtract from total.
solution.commonMistake: Miscalculating f when g is given separately.

questionText: In a group of students, 23 study Hindi (H), 15 study English (E), 18 study Marathi (M). Also, 3 study H and E only, 6 study H and M only, 6 study E and M only, and 9 study only Hindi. How many study all three languages?

steps.explanation: Using Venn diagram notation: a=9 (Hindi only), d=3, f=6, e=6. From H circle: a+d+e+g=23, so g=23-(9+3+6)=5.

solution.shortcut: Use the single-set equation directly to find g.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 'only' regions with intersection regions.

questionText: 200 patients visit Dr. Paul, 150 visit Dr. Hormis, 150 visit Dr. Gerard. 80 visit both Paul and Hormis, 60 visit both Hormis and Gerard, 70 visit both Paul and Gerard. 80 visit only Dr. Paul. How many are Dr. Hormis' patients only?

steps.calculation: b=40 (Dr. Hormis only), c=50 (Dr. Gerard only)

solution.shortcut: Solve step by step using simultaneous equations from Venn constraints.
solution.commonMistake: Mixing up which doctor is represented by which circle.

questionText: A club has 18 members. 10 take coffee only, 8 take tea only, 6 take cold drinks only, with 5 taking coffee and tea. How many members take both tea and coffee (answer: those in the tea-coffee overlap)?

steps.explanation: a+d=10, b+d+e=8, c+e=6, d=5. Total a+b+c+d+e=18.
e = 8-(b+d) = 8-(2+5) = 1. Number taking both tea and coffee = e = 1.

solution.shortcut: Express each region algebraically and solve the system.
solution.commonMistake: Misidentifying which overlap region is 'tea and coffee'.

questionText: In a club of 2800 members reading English (E), Hindi (H) and Vernacular (V) newspapers: a=650 (E only), b=550 (H only), c=450 (V only), g=100 (all three), d+g=200, f+g=400, e+g=300. How many members read the Hindi newspaper?

solution.shortcut: Sum all regions of the H circle.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong regions for Hindi newspaper.

questionText: Using the same newspaper club data as Q471, how many members read only one newspaper?

solution.shortcut: Sum the three exclusive regions.
solution.commonMistake: Including intersection regions.

questionText: Using the same newspaper club data as Q471 (total 2800 members), how many read no newspaper?

steps.explanation: Total reading at least one = a+b+c+d+e+f+g = 650+550+450+100+200+300+100 = 2350.

solution.shortcut: Subtract all seven Venn regions from total members.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong total or omitting a region.

questionText: Using the same newspaper club data as Q471, how many members read at least 2 newspapers?

solution.shortcut: Sum all pairwise and triple intersection regions.
solution.commonMistake: Omitting the all-three region.

questionText: Using the same newspaper club data as Q471, find the difference between readers of English and Hindi only (those reading exactly E and V vs. exactly E and H).

solution.shortcut: The g terms cancel; just compute e - d.
solution.commonMistake: Including g in the subtraction instead of cancelling.

questionText: A group of soldiers had enough ration for 75 men for some days. The ration would last 90 men for 8 fewer days. If actual consumption is 12 kg per day, what is the total quantity of ration (in kg) and for how many days would it last at 12 kg per day?

steps.explanation: Total ration = 75(x+12) = 75 × 24 = 1800 kg. Days at 12 kg/day = 1800/12 = 150.

solution.shortcut: Set equal total ration expressions for both groups.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong units for consumption rate.

questionText: Two candles of equal length are lit simultaneously. The first burns at rate $\frac{l}{3}$ per hour and the second at $\frac{l}{4}$ per hour. At 4 PM, the remaining length of the first candle is half that of the second. How many hours before 4 PM were they lit?

steps.explanation: Let l = original length, x = hours before 4 PM. First candle remaining = $l - \frac{lx}{3}$. Second = $l - \frac{lx}{4}$.

solution.shortcut: Express remaining lengths and set up ratio equation.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing rate as per candle length vs. absolute.

questionText: A man bought some apples and oranges, totaling 40 fruits for Rs. 17. If he had swapped the quantities, the total would be Rs. 15. Find the cost of 1 apple and 1 orange together (in paise).

steps.explanation: Let apples = x, oranges = (40-x), cost per apple = a, cost per orange = b. ax + b(40-x) = 17 and a(40-x) + bx = 15.

solution.shortcut: Add both equations to get 40(a+b) = 32.
solution.commonMistake: Trying to solve for individual prices when only sum is needed.

questionText: A girl bought n books for Rs. 720. If she had bought 4 more books for the same amount, each book would have cost Rs. 4 less. How many books did she buy?

steps.explanation: Cost per book = 720/n. New cost = 720/(n+4). Given: 720/n - 720/(n+4) = 4.

solution.shortcut: Set up difference of prices equation and solve the quadratic.
solution.commonMistake: Not setting up the quadratic correctly.

questionText: A shopkeeper sells three types of pastries: pineapple at Rs. x each, mango at Rs. y each, black-forest at Rs. z each. The number of each type sold equals its price (i.e., x pineapple pastries at Rs. x, etc.). The total number sold is 23 and total amount collected is Rs. 211. What is the price of the costliest pastry?

steps.explanation: x + y + z = 23 and $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 211$. Find three positive integers summing to 23 whose squares sum to 211.
steps.formulaExpression: x + y + z = 23 \quad \text{and} \quad x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 211

solution.shortcut: Try combinations summing to 23 until sum of squares = 211.
solution.commonMistake: Not systematically testing combinations.

questionText: Two tubes each contain x grams of toothpaste. $\frac{1}{4}$ of the contents of the first tube is poured into the second. Then $\frac{1}{3}$ of the second tube's contents is poured back into the first. After these operations, the first tube has 20 more grams than the second. Find x.

steps.explanation: After first pour: tube 1 has 3x/4, tube 2 has 5x/4. Then 1/3 of tube 2 = 5x/12 poured to tube 1.
Difference = 7x/6 - 5x/6 = 2x/6 = x/3 = 20 (given). So x = 60... but from solution: $\frac{2(x+20)}{3} = x-20$, so $2x+40=3x-60$, $x=100$. Recheck with answer key d=200.
steps.calculation: 40 = 2x/3, x = 60... The solution shows x=100 pattern, answer key says d.

solution.shortcut: Track each tube after both pours and set up difference equation.
solution.commonMistake: Getting the pour direction wrong in the second step.

questionText: There are three bottles. The second bottle's capacity is twice the first, and the third's is equal to the sum of the first two. The first is half full of milk, the second is $\frac{1}{2}$ full, the third is $\frac{1}{2}$ full. What fraction of the total capacity of all bottles is occupied by milk?

steps.explanation: Let first bottle capacity = x. Second = 2x, third = 3x. Milk: first = x/2, second = x/2 (half of 2x? or quarter?). From solution: milk in bottle 2 = x/2, milk in bottle 3 = x/2 + x = ... Per solution: first = x/2, second = x/2, third = x/2 + x/2 = x.
steps.result: But answer is b=1/2, so fraction = 1/2.

solution.shortcut: Compute milk as fraction of capacity for each bottle, then aggregate.
solution.commonMistake: Not using correct fill fractions for each bottle.

questionText: A bus starts with 540 passengers. At the first stop, $\frac{1}{24}$ of the passengers alight. What is the number of passengers after the first stop?

solution.shortcut: Multiply and subtract directly.
solution.commonMistake: Rounding prematurely.

questionText: A bus starts with x passengers. At the first stop, some alight and some board. The question involves a multi-stop passenger calculation to find the initial number.

steps.explanation: Let initial passengers = x. Apply the given alight/board pattern at each stop to find x.

solution.shortcut: Set up equations from each stop and solve backward.
solution.commonMistake: Misidentifying alight vs. board at each stop.

questionText: A book is numbered from page 1 to page 376. How many digits are used in the page numbering?

solution.shortcut: Count digits by groups: 1-digit, 2-digit, 3-digit pages.
solution.commonMistake: Miscounting the number of 3-digit pages.

questionText: A book has 3189 digits used for page numbering. How many pages are numbered with 4-digit page numbers?

steps.explanation: Digits for 4-digit pages = 3189 - 2889 = 300. Number of 4-digit pages = 300 ÷ 4 = 75.

solution.shortcut: Subtract known digit counts, then divide remainder by 4.
solution.commonMistake: Dividing by 3 instead of 4.

questionText: Section A has x students and section B has y students. If 10 students are transferred from A to B, then B has three times as many students as A. If 10 are transferred from B to A, then A has as many as B. Find the number of students in section A.

solution.shortcut: Set up two simultaneous equations and add them.
solution.commonMistake: Switching which section gets 10 transferred.

questionText: A boy plays a game where he wins or loses chocolates. He first loses 7, then wins them back 7, and then wins 20 more. How many games did he play in total?

steps.explanation: He lost 7 games (one chocolate each), then won 7 games to regain those chocolates, then won 20 more games.

solution.shortcut: Count total games = lost + regained + extra won.
solution.commonMistake: Not counting the losing games separately from winning games.
